title: The Open Risk Facing UK Universities: A Lack of AI Sovereignty
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

Originally published in a shortened version on 11 March 2025 by Research Professional News

Universities make a uniquely important contribution to the UK’s research intensive economy, both through generating new ideas and insights through basic research, and then through their application, for example in the form of research–industry collaborations, spinouts and more.

In order to maximise productivity, like almost every other sector, the research sector is looking at how to make the most of AI technologies. Closely connected to this, UK researchers are also working to develop new models and applications of AI that can support numerous sectors of the economy including the NHS, the environment, agriculture etc.

In this context, as we see with the ongoing furore around Palantir, it is a cause for concern that we are so dependent on US technology

title: Cyprus Workshop Report: The Secondary Publication Right
content:
Blog post by Giorgos Glossiotis

On Monday, 16 March 2026, KR21 organised a workshop, with the support of the Cyprus Association of Librarians – Information Scientists (CALIS, ΚΕΒΕΠ), titled “The Secondary Publication Right: Concept, Legal Framework, and the Bulgarian Experience”, which was held in Nicosia, at the University of Cyprus Library. The event was led by Ana Lazarova, KR21 national coordinator for Bulgaria.

Following a warm welcome from CALIS President Theodora Petrou, participants were introduced to KR21’s new campaign, “Let Libraries be Libraries,” by Giorgos Glossiotis, KR21 national coordinator for Greece and Cyprus.

Deep Dive: Secondary Publishing Rights & the Bulgarian Experience

title: 14 April Webinar | Closed Licences, Closed Doors: Open Licensing, AI, and the Future of Research Impact
content:
When?

  • Date: 14 April 2026
  • Time: 11:00 – 12:30 CEST / 10:00 – 11:30 BST

What?

Open licensing and copyright are important aspects of publishing research, but what do they mean in practice? Creative Commons (CC) licences offer a standardised framework for sharing research outputs, with open access being increasingly mandated by international funders. However, growing concern about research outputs being used to train generative AI tools has led some authors to seek more restrictive licences. Yet the question remains: Does a more restrictive licence stop AI from using your work, or does it simply limit the collaborations driving innovation?

Europe PMC, partners of EMBL-EBI, and KR21 invite you to a joint webinar tackling this pressing question.

Save Your Spot!

title: Portuguese Civil Society Proposes Secondary Publication Rights and Obligation
content:
Blog post by Eduardo Leonardo dos Santos

On 5 February, the magnificent Great Hall of the prestigious Academy of Sciences of Lisbon hosted Portugal’s first conference in Portugal on Secondary Publication Rights (SPR). Not even an extraordinary storm that struck Lisbon for several days could prevent an afternoon of lively discussion on the role legislation should play in advancing Open Access to publicly funded scientific outputs.

The event was organised by civil society organisations Knowledge Rights 21, COMMUNIA, and the Portuguese Association of Librarians, Archivists and Information and Documentation Professionals, alongside three Universities: NOVA University Lisbon, Coimbra University, and Minho University, with support from OPERAS and the Academy of Sciences of Lisbon. The conference featured speakers from the European Commission, UNESCO, and academic experts.
